content.problemStatement:
An array BBB of length MMM is called good if, when it is sorted, BiB_iBi​ divides Bi+1B_{i+1}Bi+1​ for every 1≤i<M1 \leq i \lt M1≤i<M.

For example, [1,2,1][1, 2, 1][1,2,1], [66,6,3][66, 6, 3][66,6,3], and [4,4][4, 4][4,4] are good arrays, while [1,2,3][1, 2, 3][1,2,3] and [99,6,3][99, 6, 3][99,6,3] are not.

You're given an array AAA containing NNN integers. Answer QQQ queries of the following form:


Given LLL and RRR, let BBB be the subarray of AAA from index LLL to index RRR, i.e, B=[AL,AL+1,…,AR]B = [A_L, A_{L+1}, \ldots, A_R]B=[AL​,AL+1​,…,AR​].

Find the number of good non-empty **subarrays** of BBB.

More formally, find the number of pairs (l,r)(l, r)(l,r) such that L≤l≤r≤RL \leq l \leq r \leq RL≤l≤r≤R and the array [Al,Al+1,…,Ar][A_l, A_{l+1}, \ldots, A_r][Al​,Al+1​,…,Ar​] is good.

Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and QQQ — the size of the array and the number of queries.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.
The next QQQ lines describe the queries. The iii-th of these QQQ lines contains two space-separated integers LiL_iLi​ and RiR_iRi​ — the parameters of the iii-th query.




Output Format
For each test case, print QQQ lines. The iii-th line should contain the answer to the iii-th query.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 150
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: first line contains two integers N and Q. The second line contains N integers A1 … AN. Then Q lines follow, each with two integers L and R (1‑based indices) describing a query.
content.outputFormat: For each query, output a single line with the number of non‑empty subarrays of A[L..R] that are good.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For each query we enumerate all subarrays inside the given range and count those whose sorted version forms a divisibility chain. The counts match the provided numbers.

solution.approach: For each query we enumerate all O((R‑L+1)^2) subarrays inside the range. For a candidate subarray we copy its elements, sort them and verify that every consecutive pair satisfies a[i] divides a[i+1]. If the check passes we increment the answer. Because N ≤ 15 the total work per test case is at most O(N^3) ≈ 3375 operations, easily fast enough.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * Q * N^2 * log N) – sorting each subarray of length ≤ N costs O(N log N), but with N ≤ 15 this is negligible.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) – temporary array for sorting each subarray.
